title: Let Your Graph Do the Talking: Encoding Structured Data for LLMs
claim: GraphToken: a GNN trained against a frozen LLM emits soft tokens encoding graph structure. Abstract headline: 'across the board improvements - up to 73% points - on node, edge and graph-level tasks from the GraphQA benchmark' — the abstract does NOT name the baseline for the headline figure. Verified table-level fragments (Table 1): edge existence 0.738 vs soft-prompt 0.544; node degree 0.962 vs soft-prompt 0.098; cycle check 0.956 vs zero-shot 0.760. Trainable side: GNN encoder body 17K-199K params + ~11M-param projector. Envelope: algorithmic reasoning tasks over small synthetic graphs.
venue_note: no peer-reviewed venue found; treat as arXiv
anchor: abstract; Table 1; parameter counts per paper text (cross-checked against kb/eval/spot-audit-2026-07-09.json)
audit_conflict: KB record arxiv:2402.05862 is SPOT-REFUTED (kb/eval/spot-audit-2026-07-09.json) for assigning the 73-point headline to a soft-prompt baseline the paper does not support, and for an unsupported kv-injection tag. This ledger entry restates the headline baseline-free for that reason.

title: G-Retriever: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Textual Graph Understanding and Question Answering
claim: PCST subgraph retrieval + GNN soft prompt. Frozen-LLM vs prompt tuning (Table 3): ExplaGraphs 0.8516 vs 0.5763, SceneGraphs 0.8131 vs 0.6341, WebQSP Hit@1 70.49 vs 48.34 — the paper's 28.23-47.77% figures are RELATIVE improvements (absolute: +17.9 to +27.5 accuracy points; +22.15 Hit@1 points). With LoRA, deltas shrink to +1.95%/+11.74%/+10.44% (relative). WebQSP ablation (Hit@1 from 70.49): w/o graph encoder 54.62 (-22.51% relative, -15.87 pts); w/o textualized graph 56.96 (-19.19% relative, -13.53 pts) — the paper frames these as complementary components; COMPONENT CONTRIBUTION only, not causal use of topology (encoder also consumes textual node/edge attributes; no edge/label shuffle run). Retrieval cuts input tokens 83-99%. WebQSP graphs avg ~1,371 nodes.
venue_note: confirmed at neurips.cc virtual poster page
anchor: Table 3 (main results, relative-% rows); WebQSP ablation table; retrieval-efficiency table

title: Graph Neural Prompting with Large Language Models
claim: GNN + cross-modality pooling + projector as plug-in prompt for FLAN-T5 hosts: +13.5% average over prompt tuning with LLM frozen and +1.8% over plain LoRA when tuned — both RELATIVE improvements, averaged across six commonsense/biomedical datasets. Absolute aggregate accuracy (Table 1): 68.55 -> 77.84 for FLAN-T5-xxlarge 11B (+9.29 pts); 65.95 -> 74.36 for FLAN-T5-xlarge 3B (+8.41 pts).

title: QA-GNN: Reasoning with Language Models and Knowledge Graphs for Question Answering
claim: LM-scored KG node relevance + joint QA-context/KG graph message passing; improves over fine-tuned LMs and prior LM+KG models on CommonsenseQA/OpenBookQA; more robust negation handling. Abstract-level; specific deltas not restated here.

title: GreaseLM: Graph REASoning Enhanced Language Models for Question Answering
claim: Multi-layer bidirectional LM-GNN fusion. CSQA 74.2% vs RoBERTa-L 68.7% (+5.5 pts) and vs QA-GNN 73.4% (+0.9 pts); OBQA 84.8% vs AristoRoBERTa 78.4% (+6.4 pts) and vs QA-GNN 82.8% (+2.0 pts); ConceptNet subgraphs pruned to top-200 nodes; competitive with models 8x (T5-3B) larger on OBQA.

title: Deep Bidirectional Language-Knowledge Graph Pretraining
claim: DRAGON: self-supervised bidirectional text-KG pretraining (MLM + link prediction); +5% absolute average over prior LM and LM+KG models; +10% on questions involving long contexts or multi-step reasoning; +8% low-resource OBQA/RiddleSense. Envelope: gains include the KG-linked pretraining compute (uncharged in comparisons).

title: KBLaM: Knowledge Base augmented Language Model
claim: MECHANISM/SCALING (primary-verified): KB triples -> continuous KV pairs via pre-trained sentence encoder + trained linear adapters, read through rectangular attention; overhead linear in KB size (vs quadratic for in-context); >10K triples into an 8B LLM with 8K window on one A100 80GB; dynamic KB updates without retraining; retrieval-free; interpretable attention. LIMITATIONS (secondary-only, UNVERIFIED): precision-decay-with-KB-size / refusal drift, OOD (Enron) degradation, and rewording drift are restated from uncited secondary sources (repo documentation, reviews) and were NOT re-verified in the paper body — direction-only, do not build on specifics.
claims_verified: mixed — mechanism/scaling primary (proceedings abstract page); limitation claims secondary-only, UNVERIFIED
anchor: ICLR proceedings abstract page (mechanism claims); limitations have NO primary anchor

title: AtlasKV: Augmenting LLMs with Billion-Scale Knowledge Graphs in 20GB VRAM
claim: Scales KBLaM-style KV injection to billion-triple KGs under ~20GB VRAM via KG2KV + HiKVP hierarchical pruning; sub-linear time and memory; no external retriever, long-context prior, or retraining for new knowledge. Abstract-level; no accuracy-vs-baseline numbers restated.
venue_note: upgraded 2026-07-11 from 'stated on arXiv page' to confirmed: camera-ready on OpenReview (forum 6i1jVAYbHs) and listed in iclr.cc 2026 materials; independent 2026-07-11 review corroborates

title: Learning to Deceive Knowledge Graph Augmented Models via Targeted Perturbation
claim: An RL policy (or simple heuristics) produces TARGETED perturbed KGs that maintain downstream performance while significantly deviating from the original KG's semantics and structure, on earlier KG-augmented commonsense-QA and recommendation models — raising doubts that these models reason about KG information. SCOPE: targeted learned/heuristic perturbations, NOT a uniform shuffled-edge control; GreaseLM/DRAGON postdate the paper and were not tested. Supports: KG-perturbation controls have published bite; content-attribution cannot be assumed. Does NOT support: that the uniform shuffle passes, or that the whole co-attention lineage's wins are unattributed.
venue_note: venue per OpenReview/proceedings listing; forum fetch blocked 2026-07-11
anchor: abstract; experimental setup (model list, perturbation methods)

title: Talk like a Graph: Encoding Graphs for Large Language Models
claim: First comprehensive study of graph-as-text encoding for LLMs: performance varies with encoding function, task, and graph structure itself; correct encoder choice boosts graph-reasoning accuracy by 4.8% to 61.8% depending on task.
venue_note: confirmed at iclr.cc virtual poster page

title: Can Language Models Solve Graph Problems in Natural Language?
claim: NLGraph benchmark: 29,370 problems, 8 graph tasks. LLMs are 37-58% above random on easy tasks (connectivity, cycle, shortest path); benefits of advanced prompting/ICL diminish on more complex problems; brittle to spurious correlations; Build-a-Graph and Algorithmic prompting recover only +3.07-16.85%.

title: CLUTRR: A Diagnostic Benchmark for Inductive Reasoning from Text
claim: Procedural kinship-story generator with parametric clause length, systematic splits (held-out rule combinations) and noise facts; substantial gap between NLU models (BERT, MAC) over text and a GNN over symbolic graph inputs — graph model shows both stronger systematic generalization and greater robustness. Licence CC-BY-NC-4.0 (per reports/lit-eval-benchmarks.md).

title: Transformers as Soft Reasoners over Language
claim: RuleTaker: transformers trained on natural-language facts+rules emulate deductive reasoning: 99% accuracy at trained depths, 95%+ on test questions requiring deeper inference chains than training, zero-shot transfer to hand-authored and paraphrased rulebases. Precedent for depth-controlled procedural benchmark generation.

title: The third AI summer: AAAI Robert S. Engelmore Memorial Lecture
claim: The canonical six-type neurosymbolic integration taxonomy (symbolic-I/O neural; Symbolic[Neuro]; Neuro|Symbolic; Neuro:Symbolic->Neuro; Neuro_Symbolic; Neuro[Symbolic]).
venue_note: bibliographic record confirmed via Wiley listing
claims_verified: secondary-only — Wiley full text returned HTTP 402 on two fetch attempts 2026-07-11; type wording restated from consistent secondary sources; do not quote as verbatim Kautz

title: Neuro-Symbolic AI in 2024: A Systematic Review
claim: PRISMA review of 2020-2024 NSAI: 1,428 papers screened, 167 analysed; concentration in learning-and-inference (63%), knowledge representation (44%), logic-and-reasoning (35%); explainability/trustworthiness 28%; meta-cognition least explored (5%).

title: Neurosymbolic AI: The 3rd Wave
claim: Agenda paper: integrating neural learning with symbolic knowledge representation and logical reasoning for trust, safety, interpretability, accountability; surveys 20 years of neural-symbolic computing. Abstract-level only; its restatement of Kautz's taxonomy was not extractable from the fetched PDF.

title: Large Language Models on Graphs: A Comprehensive Survey
claim: Field taxonomy for LLM-graph work: scenarios {pure graphs, text-attributed graphs, text-paired graphs} x techniques {LLM as Predictor, LLM as Encoder, LLM as Aligner}.

title: GraphGPT: Graph Instruction Tuning for Large Language Models
claim: Text-graph grounding + dual-stage graph instruction tuning + lightweight graph-text alignment projector; claims superior supervised and zero-shot graph-task generalization without downstream graph data.

title: LLaGA: Large Language and Graph Assistant
claim: Reorganizes graph nodes into structure-aware sequences mapped into token space by a versatile projector — NO GNN; one model performs across four datasets and three tasks, surpassing graph models in supervised and zero-shot settings, generalizing to unseen datasets/tasks. Evidence that templates+projector deliver much of what a graph encoder buys.

title: Can we Soft Prompt LLMs for Graph Learning Tasks?
claim: GraphPrompter: GNN-encoded soft prompts adapt frozen LLMs for node classification and link prediction; qualitative claim that LLMs can be effectively soft-prompted for graph tasks; zero-shot LLM performance on these tasks is poor.

title: On the Bottleneck of Graph Neural Networks and its Practical Implications
claim: Over-squashing: exponentially growing receptive-field information compressed into fixed-size vectors prevents GNNs from propagating long-range information; GCN/GIN more susceptible than attention-based GNNs; relieving the bottleneck improves long-range tasks without extra parameters. Names the GNN-side depth ceiling for any deep-composition fusion cell.

title: Towards Neural Scaling Laws on Graphs
claim: On GNNs/graph transformers up to 100M params and 50M samples: model depth affects scaling beyond parameter count (unlike vision/NLP); data volume better measured in nodes/edges than graph count; reformed scaling law fits node/link/graph tasks.
venue_note: no venue found; treat as arXiv

title: GNN-RAG: Graph Neural Retrieval for Large Language Model Reasoning
claim: GNN as dense-subgraph reasoner extracts KG reasoning paths, verbalised for the LLM: tuned 7B LLaMA2 matches or exceeds GPT-4 on WebQSP and CWQ; +8.9-15.5 points on multi-hop and multi-entity questions. Precedent for the proposed H-GNN-RET variant (structure consumed before the boundary; text crosses).
venue_note: no peer-reviewed venue found; treat as arXiv

title: Lost in Serialization: Invariance and Generalization of LLM Graph Reasoners
venue: ICML 2026 Workshop on Graph Foundation Models (stated by source only)
claim: LLM graph reasoners are not invariant to meaning-preserving re-serialisation (node reindexing, edge reordering, formatting); larger non-fine-tuned models more robust; fine-tuning reduces label sensitivity but increases structural/format sensitivity and does not consistently improve unseen-task performance. Mandates a serialisation-invariance battery for any text-serialisation control arm.
venue_note: workshop venue stated on page; not confirmed against programme

title: Revisiting Graph-Tokenizing Large Language Models: A Systematic Evaluation of Graph Token Understanding
claim: GTEval: graph-token LLMs do not fully understand graph tokens — up to ~50% drops under instruction rephrasing, <10% on content-level variants; under PRBCD structural attack text-carrying fused models are almost unchanged while GCN collapses (-73.65%), showing reliance on text not structure; yet probes show graph tokens CONTAIN task-relevant structural signal (link prediction from graph tokens ~ from original embeddings) that the LLM fails to use. Field-level replication of the delivery-without-integration failure mode.
venue_note: venue stated on arXiv HTML; independent 2026-07-11 review also found no programme confirmation
anchor: abstract; PRBCD attack results; probing section

title: ERNIE: Enhanced Language Representation with Informative Entities
claim: KG entity information fused into BERT-class encoders using corpora+KGs jointly; significant improvements on knowledge-driven tasks while comparable with BERT on common NLP tasks. Anchor of the KEPLM weight-fusion lineage (verdict on the lineage inherited from reports/lit-llm-injection-priorart.md).
